examples.title: Eucalyptus VM Instance Launch Commands
examples.description: Commands to register an image, create key pair, and launch a VM instance in Eucalyptus
examples.code:
# Register a machine image
eucalyptus-register -m centos6.4-x86_64 -n centos-image

# Bundle the image for upload
eucalyptus-bundle-image -i centos6.4-x86_64.xz --ramdisk none --kernel eukernel

# Upload bundled image to Walrus
eucalyptus-upload-bundle -b mybucket -m /var/tmp/centos6.4-x86_64.manifest.xml

# Create a key pair for SSH access
euca-create-keypair mykey > mykey.pem
chmod 400 mykey.pem

# Create security group
euca-create-group -n web-servers -d "Web server instances"

euca-authorize -P tcp -p 80 -s 0.0.0.0/0 web-servers

# Run an instance
euca-run-instances -k mykey -t m1.small emi-12345678
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the workflow to launch a VM in Eucalyptus: registering an EMI, bundling/uploading to Walrus storage, creating authentication key pair, configuring security group with firewall rules, and finally running the instance.

examples.title: Eucalyptus Cloud Controller Scheduling Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how CLC schedules VM instances to appropriate Node Controllers
examples.code:
ALGORITHM ScheduleInstance
INPUT: instanceType, imageID, availabilityZone
OUTPUT: selectedNodeController

BEGIN
  GET availableNCs FROM clusterController
  SET eligibleNCs = []
  
  FOR EACH nc IN availableNCs DO
    IF nc.hasCapacity(instanceType) THEN
      IF availabilityZone = "ANY" OR nc.zone = availabilityZone THEN
        ADD nc TO eligibleNCs
      END IF
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  IF eligibleNCs IS EMPTY THEN
    RETURN FAILURE("No available resources")
  END IF
  
  // Select NC based on scheduling policy
  SWITCH schedulingPolicy OF
    CASE "round-robin":
      selectedNC = roundRobinSelect(eligibleNCs)
    CASE "best-fit":
      selectedNC = findNCWithMostResources(eligibleNCs)
    CASE "first-fit":
      selectedNC = eligibleNCs[0]
  END SWITCH
  
  RETURN selectedNC
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm shows how the Cloud Controller schedules instances: it collects eligible Node Controllers with sufficient capacity, filters by availability zone, and selects a node based on the configured scheduling policy (round-robin, best-fit, or first-fit).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of Node Controllers

examples.title: Eucalyptus API Python Example
examples.description: Using Eucalyptus AWS-compatible API with Boto to manage instances
examples.code:
import boto
from boto.ec2 import EC2Connection

# Connect to Eucalyptus cloud (AWS-compatible API)
conn = EC2Connection(
    aws_access_key_id='YOUR_ACCESS_KEY',
    aws_secret_access_key='YOUR_SECRET_KEY',
    api_version='2014-02-01',
    region='eucalyptus',  # Eucalyptus region
    host='cloud-controller-ip',
    port=8773,
    path='/services/Eucalyptus'
)

# List available instances
reservations = conn.get_all_instances()
for res in reservations:
    for inst in res.instances:
        print(f"Instance: {inst.id}, State: {inst.state}")

# Create and run a new instance
ami_id = 'emi-12345678'
key_name = 'mykey'
instance_type = 'm1.small'
security_groups = ['web-servers']

reservation = conn.run_instances(
    image_id=ami_id,
    instance_type=instance_type,
    key_name=key_name,
    security_groups=security_groups
)

print(f"Launched instance: {reservation.instances[0].id}")
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates using Boto library with Eucalyptus's AWS-compatible EC2 API. The same code can work with both AWS and Eucalyptus by changing the host/port, making it easy to migrate applications between private and public clouds.

examples.title: Walrus Storage Operations
examples.description: Pseudocode for storing and retrieving machine images in Walrus object storage
examples.code:
ALGORITHM UploadImageToWalrus
INPUT: imagePath, bucketName
OUTPUT: manifestLocation

BEGIN
  // Step 1: Split image into parts
  SET parts = splitIntoParts(imagePath, PART_SIZE=10MB)
  
  // Step 2: Upload parts to Walrus bucket
  FOR i FROM 0 TO parts.length-1 DO
    partKey = CONCAT(bucketName, "/", imageName, ".part-", i)
    walrus.putObject(partKey, parts[i])
  END FOR
  
  // Step 3: Create and upload manifest
  manifest = createManifest(imageName, parts, checksum)
  manifestKey = CONCAT(bucketName, "/", imageName, ".manifest.xml")
  walrus.putObject(manifestKey, manifest)
  
  RETURN manifestKey
END

ALGORITHM DownloadImageFromWalrus
INPUT: bucketName, manifestLocation
OUTPUT: imageData

BEGIN
  manifest = walrus.getObject(manifestLocation)
  parts = manifest.partList
  
  imageData = ""
  FOR EACH part IN parts DO
    partData = walrus.getObject(bucketName + "/" + part.key)
    imageData = CONCAT(imageData, partData)
  END FOR
  
  RETURN imageData
END
examples.explanation: These algorithms show how Eucalyptus's Walrus (S3-compatible storage) handles machine images: it splits large images into parts for efficient upload, creates a manifest for tracking, and reconstructs the image when needed by Node Controllers.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of parts
